Q1.bottleneck: String-based GROUP BY with std::unordered_map<std::string, ...> - extremely slow hashing and comparisons on returnflag/linestatus strings
Q1.root_cause: l_returnflag and l_linestatus are stored as std::vector<std::string> instead of dictionary-encoded integers. Q1 creates 59M string copies during scan, then uses std::string as hash key (expensive hashing). Only 4 distinct groups but 59M string hash operations.
Q1.benchmark_gap: 13.6x slower than DuckDB (2706ms vs 199ms). DuckDB uses dictionary encoding and SIMD-vectorized aggregation.

Q3.bottleneck: Full table scans on orders (15M rows) and lineitem (60M rows) without zone maps or sorted data - no block pruning despite highly selective date filters
Q3.root_cause: Storage design specifies sorted layout on l_shipdate and o_orderdate with zone maps, but implementation doesn't actually sort data during load. Q3 scans 100% of lineitem and orders when zone maps on sorted data could skip 70-80% of blocks. Additionally, c_mktsegment filter uses string comparison instead of dictionary-encoded integer comparison.
Q3.benchmark_gap: 16.9x slower than DuckDB (2277ms vs 135ms). DuckDB sorts/clusters data by date and uses aggressive block pruning.

Q6.bottleneck: Full lineitem scan (60M rows) without zone maps - no block skipping despite date filter (1994-01-01 to 1995-01-01) that should eliminate ~86% of data
Q6.root_cause: Same as Q3: data not sorted by l_shipdate, no zone maps implemented. Q6 is a pure filter query with tight loop - should be fastest but scans all data. Additionally, no SIMD vectorization on the tight filter loop.
Q6.benchmark_gap: 9.5x slower than DuckDB (256ms vs 27ms). DuckDB uses zone maps + SIMD vectorized filters.

analysis.overall: All three queries suffer from two critical missing optimizations: (1) Data is not sorted by date columns despite storage design specification - no zone map pruning is possible. (2) String columns (returnflag, linestatus, mktsegment) are stored as std::string instead of dictionary-encoded integers - causing massive overhead in filters and GROUP BY. Additionally, no SIMD/vectorization is applied. Q3 is the slowest relative to DuckDB (16.9x) due to compounding issues: string filters + unsorted data + two large table scans.

performance_optimizations.description:
Replace std::vector<std::string> with dictionary-encoded integer storage for l_returnflag, l_linestatus, and c_mktsegment.

In storage/storage.h:
- Add dictionary structures for each low-cardinality column
- Replace std::vector<std::string> l_returnflag with std::vector<uint8_t> l_returnflag_code
- Add std::vector<std::string> l_returnflag_dict (will have 3 entries: 'A', 'N', 'R')
- Same for l_linestatus (2 values: 'F', 'O') and c_mktsegment (5 values)

In storage/storage.cpp (load functions):
- Build dictionary during load: when encountering a string, check if it exists in dict, if not add it, then store the code
- Example: auto code = encode_returnflag(fields[8], lineitem.l_returnflag_dict); lineitem.l_returnflag_code.push_back(code);

In queries/q1.cpp:
- Change Q1GroupKey to use uint8_t returnflag_code, uint8_t linestatus_code instead of std::string
- Hash function becomes trivial: return (returnflag_code << 8) | linestatus_code
- For output, decode using dictionary: lineitem.l_returnflag_dict[code]

In queries/q3.cpp:
- Change customer filter to: uint8_t building_code = encode_mktsegment("BUILDING", customer.c_mktsegment_dict); if (customer.c_mktsegment_code[i] == building_code)

Expected improvement: Q1 will improve by 3-5x (removes 59M string hashes/comparisons), Q3 by 1.5-2x (faster customer filter)
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: Q1: 2706ms -> ~600ms (4.5x speedup from eliminating string operations in hot aggregation loop). Q3: 2277ms -> ~1500ms (1.5x from faster filter, but still bottlenecked by unsorted data).
performance_optimizations.knowledge_source: storage/string-optimization.md - Dictionary Encoding section. Perfect match for low-cardinality columns (3, 2, and 5 distinct values).

performance_optimizations.technique: Sorted storage with zone maps on date columns
performance_optimizations.description:
Sort lineitem by l_shipdate during load and build zone maps (min/max per block). Same for orders by o_orderdate.

In storage/storage.h:
- Add zone map structures:
  struct ZoneMap { std::vector<int32_t> block_min_shipdate; std::vector<int32_t> block_max_shipdate; size_t block_size = 65536; };
- Add ZoneMap lineitem_zonemap to LineitemTable
- Add ZoneMap orders_zonemap to OrdersTable

In storage/storage.cpp:
- After loading all lineitem rows, sort by l_shipdate: create index vector, sort it by shipdate, then reorder all columns
- Build zone maps: for each block of 65536 rows, compute min/max shipdate and store in zonemap.block_min/max
- Same for orders table sorted by o_orderdate

In queries/q1.cpp, q3.cpp, q6.cpp:
- Before scanning lineitem, use zone map to identify blocks to skip
- Example for Q6: for (size_t block = 0; block < num_blocks; block++) { if (lineitem.zonemap.block_max_shipdate[block] < min_date || lineitem.zonemap.block_min_shipdate[block] >= max_date) continue; /* skip block */ }
- Only scan rows in non-skipped blocks

Note: Sorting is a one-time cost during load. Zone map checks add negligible overhead (<1ms) but enable skipping 70-90% of blocks.

Expected improvement: Q1 scans only ~2% more data (shipdate <= 1998-09-02 is ~98% of data, so zone maps help little). Q3 and Q6 skip 70-80% of blocks (huge win).
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: Q1: minimal improvement (~5%, still processes 98% of data). Q3: 1500ms -> ~400ms (3.75x from skipping 70% of lineitem blocks + 50% of orders blocks). Q6: 256ms -> ~40ms (6.4x from skipping 86% of blocks).
performance_optimizations.knowledge_source: indexing/zone-maps.md. Storage design specified sorted layout + zone maps but implementation didn't actually sort the data. This is the most impactful missing feature for Q3 and Q6.

performance_optimizations.technique: Specialized aggregation for ultra-low cardinality GROUP BY
performance_optimizations.description:
Replace std::unordered_map with direct-indexed array for Q1's 4-group aggregation.

In queries/q1.cpp:
- Remove std::unordered_map<Q1GroupKey, Q1Aggregates>
- Create fixed array: Q1Aggregates agg_array[6]; (max 3 returnflag * 2 linestatus = 6 combinations)
- Compute direct index: size_t idx = returnflag_code * 2 + linestatus_code;
- Update: agg_array[idx].sum_qty += quantity;
- After scan, iterate over agg_array[0..5] and output non-empty groups

This eliminates ALL hashing overhead - direct array indexing is ~10-20x faster than hash table lookup for such small cardinality.

Expected improvement: Eliminates hash table overhead entirely. Combined with dictionary encoding, Q1 becomes bottlenecked by memory bandwidth (reading 60M rows * 7 columns).
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: Q1: 600ms -> ~250ms (2.4x speedup by removing hash table overhead). Note: requires priority 1 optimization (dictionary encoding) to be implemented first.
performance_optimizations.knowledge_source: aggregation/hash-aggregation.md - Perfect Hashing for Low Cardinality section. With only 4-6 groups, direct indexing is optimal.

performance_optimizations.description:
Replace full hash aggregation + sort with a min-heap of size 10 maintained during aggregation.

In queries/q3.cpp:
- Keep the hash aggregation phase (necessary because we don't know which orders are top 10 until we sum all their lineitems)
- But instead of sorting all groups after aggregation, use std::partial_sort or maintain a min-heap during the aggregation-to-vector conversion
- After aggregation completes, use std::partial_sort(results.begin(), results.begin() + 10, results.end(), comparator) instead of full sort
- This is O(n log 10) instead of O(n log n) where n = ~50K-100K groups

Expected improvement: Reduces sort overhead from ~10-20ms to ~2-3ms. Modest but worthwhile for Top-K queries.

Alternatively, for more advanced optimization: during aggregation, maintain a min-heap of size 10 tracking the current top 10. When inserting a new group, if revenue > heap.top(), pop heap and insert new group. This is more complex but avoids materializing all groups.

For iteration 1, recommend the simpler std::partial_sort approach.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: Q3: 400ms -> ~385ms (modest ~4% improvement). Sort is not the bottleneck - data scanning is. But this is a standard Top-K optimization and very low risk.
performance_optimizations.knowledge_source: Novel technique based on standard Top-K heap optimization (mentioned in workload_analysis.json). Standard database technique for ORDER BY ... LIMIT queries.

performance_optimizations.technique: Manual loop unrolling and expression simplification
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: Q6: 256ms -> ~230ms (modest 10% improvement from better code generation). After zone maps: 40ms -> ~36ms.
performance_optimizations.knowledge_source: Novel - basic compiler optimization techniques. Query-execution/vectorized-execution.md discusses batch processing principles.

performance_optimizations.technique: Expression precomputation and data access pattern optimization
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: Q1: 250ms -> ~200ms (1.25x from better code generation). Approaching DuckDB performance (199ms).
performance_optimizations.knowledge_source: Novel - standard compiler optimization techniques. Storage/memory-layout.md discusses cache-friendly access patterns.

storage_changes.change: Sort by l_shipdate during load, build zone maps with 65K row blocks. Replace l_returnflag and l_linestatus std::string vectors with uint8_t code vectors + dictionaries.
storage_changes.rationale: Zone maps enable skipping 70-90% of blocks for date-filtered queries (Q3, Q6). Dictionary encoding eliminates string overhead in Q1 GROUP BY (4.5x faster). Sorting is one-time cost during load.

storage_changes.change: Sort by o_orderdate during load, build zone maps with 65K row blocks.
storage_changes.rationale: Q3 filters orders by date (< 1995-03-15). Zone maps can skip ~50-60% of blocks. Critical for closing gap with DuckDB on Q3.

storage_changes.change: Replace c_mktsegment std::string vector with uint8_t code vector + dictionary (5 values).
storage_changes.rationale: Q3 filters by c_mktsegment = 'BUILDING'. Dictionary encoding enables integer comparison (5-10x faster than string comparison) for 1.5M customer rows.

summary: Iteration 1 baseline is functional but missing two critical optimizations from storage design: (1) Dictionary encoding for low-cardinality strings - causing massive overhead in Q1 GROUP BY and Q3 filters. (2) Sorted data + zone maps - preventing block pruning despite highly selective date filters. Priority 1 and 2 optimizations (dictionary encoding + zone maps) are CRITICAL and should both be implemented. Together they will close the gap with DuckDB: Q1 from 13.6x slower to ~1x, Q3 from 16.9x slower to ~3x, Q6 from 9.5x slower to ~1.3x. Remaining gap after these optimizations will be due to lack of SIMD vectorization and advanced techniques. Recommend implementing optimizations 1-3 (dictionary encoding, zone maps, direct-indexed aggregation for Q1) in iteration 2 as they are low-risk and high-impact.
